title: Figure S1. Prevention-care pathway visibility matrix. Indicator availability across exposure, burden, diagnosis, treatment, survival, and socioeconomic outcome domains.
Figure S2. Missingness heatmap for core indicators. Countries and variables are ordered to show where complete overlap is gained or lost.
Figure S3. Missingness by WHO region and income group. Coverage patterns show the data-system dimension of equity surveillance.
Figure S4. Clean-fuel deficit, development gradients, and rural disadvantage. Clean-fuel deficit is interpreted as a structural household energy equity indicator.
Figure S5. Historical female smoking model diagnostics. First-stage smoking-adjusted models for female lung-cancer mortality, YLL, and DALY.

Figure S6. Environmental indicators and smoking-adjusted burden residuals. Clean-fuel deficit, PM2.5, and rural clean-fuel disadvantage are shown as country-level residual model predictors with HC3 robust 95% confidence intervals.
Figure S7. PM2.5 heterogeneity and influence diagnostics. Sensitivity analyses for non-high-income countries.
Figure S8. Smoking-adjusted burden residual scatter diagnostics. Scatter plots show mortality-based residual patterns against clean-fuel deficit and ambient PM2.5.
Figure S9. Mortality- and incidence-based residual consistency. Scatter plot and correlation statistics comparing residual definitions.
Figure S10. Current exposure typology summary. Summary of low, single, double, and triple exposure classifications based on top-tertile exposure thresholds.
Figure S11. Standalone exposure typology heatmap. Country-level heatmap of z-scored exposure and burden variables ordered by exposure typology.
Figure S12. Multiple-exposure score sensitivity. Equal-weighted, PCA-weighted, rank-based, and tertile-score variants are compared as transparent surveillance heuristics.
Figure S13. Illustrative country profiles for surveillance interpretation. Examples are descriptive and are not causal country assessments.
Figure S14. Sensitivity analysis summary. Summary of smoking-window, outcome replacement, exposure replacement, typology threshold, clustering, imputation, influence, and stratified analyses.
Figure S15. Lagged environmental exposure models. Historical smoking, persistent clean-fuel deficit, and PM2.5 latency windows.
Figure S16. Model performance comparison. Environmental indicators were more useful for subgroup surveillance than for uniform global residual explanation.
Figure S17. Measured exposure-burden profile matrix. This former main-text matrix is included as a supplementary descriptive surveillance figure with neutral profile labels.
